title: Cat Pupils Different Sizes? Anisocoria Guide for Pet Owners
meta_desc: Discover why your cat's pupils are different sizes (anisocoria), why it's a veterinary emergency, and how to get help immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Unequal pupil size, or anisocoria, is a high-priority medical emergency in cats that requires immediate veterinary attention. Capturing a clear photo of your cat's eyes before heading to the clinic can provide vital information for the triage team to track the condition's progression.

What is anisocoria in cats?

Anisocoria is the medical term used when a cat's pupils are two different sizes. Normally, a cat's pupils should expand and contract simultaneously in response to light. When one pupil is significantly larger or smaller than the other, it indicates that the eye, the brain, or the nerves connecting them are not functioning correctly.

Is it an emergency if my cat's pupils are different sizes?

  • Yes, different-sized pupils are considered a high-priority medical emergency.
  • This symptom can be caused by serious issues such as head trauma, glaucoma, internal eye inflammation, or neurological disorders.
  • Contact a veterinarian or an emergency animal hospital immediately.
  • Rapid treatment is often necessary to prevent permanent vision loss or to address life-threatening underlying conditions.

Why should I take a photo of my cat's eyes to help with triage?

  • In many cases, pupils can change size rapidly or may look different depending on the lighting.
  • Taking a clear, well-lit photo of your cat's eyes before you head to the clinic provides a baseline of what the eyes looked like when you first noticed the problem.
  • The photo helps the veterinary team determine if the condition is progressing or shifting during the triage process.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Anisocoria in cats, or pupils of different sizes, can be caused by various factors. Feline leukemia virus (FeLV) infection can cause anisocoria, though these cats usually show other clinical signs and are FeLV-negative. Pilocarpine (0.1%) applied to the cornea causes profound miosis within 10-15 minutes due to denervation hypersensitivity but has no effect on a healthy cat. Dilute (0.5%) phenylephrine reverses ptosis and a protruding third eyelid. Anterior uveitis, or iridocyclitis, when acute, can manifest as miosis. Iris colobomas are rare but can cause pupil irregularity.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1256)

faqs.question: Can anisocoria happen suddenly?
faqs.answer: Yes, anisocoria often appears suddenly, especially if it is caused by injury, a stroke, or acute glaucoma.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms to look for?
faqs.answer: Keep an eye out for squinting, redness in the eye, cloudiness, or changes in your cat's behavior and coordination.

faqs.question: Should I wait to see if it gets better?
faqs.answer: No, you should not wait. Delaying treatment can lead to permanent blindness or allow an underlying neurological issue to worsen.
